Agent input={"description":"Review 1: code quality audit","prompt":"You are a senior Python engineer reviewing new code in D:\\demo1\\memorytree-workflow\\scripts.\n\n7 new Python files wer...
Show input payload 
{
  "description": "Review 1: code quality audit",
  "prompt": "You are a senior Python engineer reviewing new code in D:\\demo1\\memorytree-workflow\\scripts.\n\n7 new Python files were just added to implement a heartbeat system and session continuity feature. Review ALL of them for **code quality, correctness, and potential bugs**.\n\n## Files to review\n1. `scripts/_config_utils.py` (157 lines) — config.toml loading/validation\n2. `scripts/_alert_utils.py` (97 lines) — alerts.json management\n3. `scripts/_lock_utils.py` (92 lines) — PID-based lock file\n4. `scripts/_log_utils.py` (64 lines) — logging setup\n5. `scripts/heartbeat.py` (223 lines) — heartbeat entry point\n6. `scripts/memorytree_daemon.py` (347 lines) — daemon CLI\n7. `scripts/recall-session.py` (293 lines) — on-demand sync + session recall\n\n## Review checklist\n\n1. **Correctness**: Will the code work as intended? Check for logic errors, off-by-one errors, incorrect assumptions.\n\n2. **Immutability**: The project's coding style requires immutable patterns — new objects instead of mutating existing ones. Check if any code mutates objects in place when it shouldn't.\n\n3. **Error handling**: Is every external operation (file I/O, subprocess, SQLite) wrapped in appropriate error handling? Are errors silently swallowed inappropriately?\n\n4. **Cross-platform**: The code must work on Linux, macOS, and Windows. Check:\n   - Path handling (forward/backward slashes)\n   - Process detection (os.kill vs ctypes)\n   - Subprocess calls (git, crontab, launchctl, schtasks)\n   - File locking edge cases\n\n5. **Security**: Check for command injection risks in subprocess calls, especially in memorytree_daemon.py where user-provided paths are passed to shell commands.\n\n6. **Race conditions**: Lock file acquire/release — is there a TOCTOU race between checking and writing the lock file?\n\n7. **Resource leaks**: Are file handles, SQLite connections, subprocess pipes properly closed?\n\n8. **API consistency**: Do the scripts follow the same patterns as existing scripts (discover-transcripts.py, import-transcripts.py)?\n\n9. **Function size**: Functions should be <50 lines per the coding style guide.\n\n10. **Import dependencies**: Do all imports resolve correctly? The scripts use relative imports from the same directory — will they work when invoked with `uv run python scripts/heartbeat.py`?\n\nRead ALL 7 new files. Report findings as:\n- CRITICAL: Bug that will cause crashes or data loss\n- HIGH: Significant issue\n- MEDIUM: Should fix\n- LOW: Nitpick\n\nDo NOT make any edits. Research only."
}
Tool Call #566 Agent toolu_016npBVNGV6QwGWhZqhXMPDP 03:46:32
Agent input={"description":"Review 2: spec compliance check","prompt":"You are a specification compliance reviewer for D:\\demo1\\memorytree-workflow.\n\nThe project has detailed reference ...
Show input payload 
{
  "description": "Review 2: spec compliance check",
  "prompt": "You are a specification compliance reviewer for D:\\demo1\\memorytree-workflow.\n\nThe project has detailed reference docs (specs) that define how features should behave. Code was just written to implement those specs. Your job is to verify the code **faithfully implements the specs** with no gaps or deviations.\n\n## Files to review\n\n**Specs:**\n- `references/heartbeat-scheduling.md` — heartbeat architecture spec\n- `references/global-configuration.md` — config and alerts spec\n- `TODO.md` — task definitions and acceptance criteria\n\n**Code:**\n- `scripts/heartbeat.py`\n- `scripts/memorytree_daemon.py`\n- `scripts/_config_utils.py`\n- `scripts/_alert_utils.py`\n- `scripts/_lock_utils.py`\n- `scripts/_log_utils.py`\n- `scripts/recall-session.py`\n\n## Review checklist\n\n1. **Execution Flow compliance**: heartbeat-scheduling.md defines a 6-step execution flow. Does heartbeat.py follow all 6 steps in the correct order?\n\n2. **Lock Mechanism compliance**: The spec says PID-based lock with stale detection. Does _lock_utils.py implement exactly what the spec says (check PID alive, delete stale, write current PID, try/finally cleanup)?\n\n3. **Error Handling compliance**: The spec says \"per-project isolation\", \"failure threshold 3\", \"config errors use defaults + log warning\", \"git errors skip push + alert\". Does heartbeat.py implement all of these?\n\n4. **Daemon CLI compliance**: The spec defines 5 subcommands (install/uninstall/run-once/watch/status). Does memorytree_daemon.py implement all 5? Does each behave as specified?\n\n5. **OS Scheduler compliance**: The spec defines per-platform details (cron syntax, launchd plist structure, schtasks flags). Does memorytree_daemon.py match?\n\n6. **Config.toml compliance**: The spec defines 5 fields with types and defaults. Does _config_utils.py validate each field correctly? Does it handle malformed TOML as specified?\n\n7. **Alerts.json compliance**: The spec says dedup by (project, type), max 100, display=ack, failure threshold 3. Does _alert_utils.py implement all of these?\n\n8. **On-Demand Sync compliance**: heartbeat-scheduling.md defines a 6-step on-demand sync flow. Does recall-session.py follow it? Specifically check: activation timestamp exclusion, no-match fallback message, cross-client support.\n\n9. **Sensitive Info Scanning**: The spec says scan for API keys/passwords/tokens, log warning, write alert, never delete. Does heartbeat.py implement this correctly?\n\n10. **Missing features**: Is there anything specified in the reference docs that is NOT implemented in the code?\n\nRead ALL spec files and ALL code files. Report findings as:\n- CRITICAL: Spec violation that breaks the intended behavior\n- HIGH: Significant deviation from spec\n- MEDIUM: Minor deviation\n- LOW: Nitpick\n\nDo NOT make any edits. Research only."
}
Tool Result #567 Agent toolu_011uci2Neomy4pNt9HhezVPN 03:48:21
Agent output=[{"text":"Now I have all the information I need for a thorough review. Here is my complete analysis:\n\n---\n\n## Code Review Report: Heartbeat System and Session Continuity\n\n...
Show output payload 
[
  {
    "text": "Now I have all the information I need for a thorough review. Here is my complete analysis:\n\n---\n\n## Code Review Report: Heartbeat System and Session Continuity\n\n### CRITICAL\n\n**1. `memorytree_daemon.py` lines 339-343 -- `_python_path()` returns a multi-word string used unsplit in `subprocess.run()` list arguments**\n\n```python\ndef _python_path() -> str:\n    uv = shutil.which(\"uv\")\n    if uv:\n        return f\"{uv} run python\"\n    return sys.executable\n```\n\nThis returns e.g. `\"/home/user/.local/bin/uv run python\"` as a single string. It is then used in:\n\n```python\n# line 119\nresult = subprocess.run([python, str(heartbeat_script)], ...)\n# line 133\nsubprocess.run([python, str(heartbeat_script)], ...)\n```\n\nWhen `uv` is found, this becomes `[\"/usr/bin/uv run python\", \"heartbeat.py\"]` -- the OS will try to find a literal executable named `\"uv run python\"` (with spaces) and fail with `FileNotFoundError`. The string needs to be split into `[\"uv\", \"run\", \"python\"]` before being placed in the list.\n\nThis also affects `_install_cron()` (line 187), `_install_launchd()` (line 243), and `_install_schtasks()` (line 299) where `python` is interpolated into command strings or plist XML. The cron/plist/schtasks cases happen to work for the shell-based paths because the whole command is interpreted by cron's shell or launchd, but `subprocess.run()` with a list will absolutely fail.\n\n**2. `_lock_utils.py` lines 19-35 -- TOCTOU race condition in `acquire_lock()`**\n\nBetween checking `path.exists()` on line 19 and writing the PID on line 32, another process can create the lock file. Two concurrent heartbeat instances starting simultaneously can both see \"no lock file\" and both write their PID, resulting in two heartbeats running simultaneously. This matters because the heartbeat does git commit/push operations where concurrent execution can cause conflicts.\n\nA proper fix would use `open(path, 'x')` (exclusive create) or `fcntl.flock` / `msvcrt.locking` for atomic lock acquisition.\n\n**3. `recall-session.py` lines 138-151 -- SQLite connection not closed on exception path**\n\n```python\nconn = sqlite3.connect(db_path)\nconn.row_factory = sqlite3.Row\ncursor = conn.execute(...)\nrows = cursor.fetchall()\nconn.close()\n```\n\nIf `conn.execute()` or `cursor.fetchall()` raises an exception that is not `sqlite3.Error` or `OSError` (e.g. `MemoryError`, `KeyboardInterrupt`), `conn.close()` is skipped. Should use a `with` context manager or `try/finally`. Additionally, even on the handled exception path (line 152-153), the `conn` that was successfully opened is never closed before falling through to the JSONL fallback.\n\n---\n\n### HIGH\n\n**4. `_alert_utils.py` lines 41-46 -- In-place mutation of alert dicts**\n\n```python\nfor alert in alerts:\n    if alert.get(\"project\") == project and alert.get(\"type\") == alert_type:\n        alert[\"timestamp\"] = now\n        alert[\"message\"] = message\n        alert[\"count\"] = alert.get(\"count\", 1) + 1\n```\n\nThe project's coding style explicitly requires immutable patterns. The `alert` dict (read from disk) is mutated in place. A new dict should be created and substituted into the list, or a new list should be built.\n\n**5. `memorytree_daemon.py` line 187 -- Shell injection via interpolated paths in cron line**\n\n```python\ncron_line = f\"*/{minutes} * * * * cd {heartbeat_script.parent} && {python} {heartbeat_script} >> ...\"\n```\n\nThe `heartbeat_script.parent` and `python` values are interpolated directly into a cron entry that is passed to `crontab -`. If any of these paths contain shell metacharacters (spaces, semicolons, backticks, etc.), this can break or be exploited. The paths should be shell-quoted using `shlex.quote()`.\n\nThe same issue exists in the launchd plist (line 235-256), though XML escaping is less exploitable. And in `_install_schtasks()` (line 299), the `f'\"{python}\" \"{heartbeat_script}\"'` approach partially mitigates this but would still fail if the path contains double quotes.\n\n**6. `heartbeat.py` lines 22-30 -- Import of `_transcript_utils` will fail at runtime**\n\n`heartbeat.py` does `from _transcript_utils import ...` using bare module names. There is no `__init__.py` in the `scripts/` directory (confirmed by glob), and these are not installed packages. This import style only works if the scripts are invoked with `cwd` set to the `scripts/` directory, or if `scripts/` is on `sys.path`.\n\nThe existing scripts (`discover-transcripts.py`, `import-transcripts.py`) use the same pattern and rely on `cwd=scripts/` at invocation time. The daemon's `cmd_run_once()` (line 119) passes `cwd=heartbeat_script.parent` which covers this, and `cmd_watch()` (line 133) does too. But `_install_cron()` line 187 uses `cd {heartbeat_script.parent} &&` which is correct. So this works by convention, but it's fragile -- any invocation without setting cwd will fail. This is consistent with existing scripts so marking as HIGH rather than CRITICAL.\n\n**7. `recall-session.py` line 217 -- Overly broad path matching allows false positives**\n\n```python\ndef _cwd_matches(cwd: str, resolved_root: str) -> bool:\n    ...\n    return cwd_resolved == resolved_root or resolved_root in cwd_resolved\n```\n\nThe `resolved_root in cwd_resolved` check is a substring match, not a path hierarchy check. For example, if `resolved_root` is `/home/user/app` and `cwd_resolved` is `/home/user/app-backup/subdir`, this would return `True` as a false positive. Should use `cwd_resolved.startswith(resolved_root + \"/\")` or proper `Path.parents` logic.\n\n---\n\n### MEDIUM\n\n**8. `_config_utils.py` lines 115-134 -- `save_config` does not escape special characters in TOML values**\n\n```python\nlines.append(f'path = \"{project.path}\"')\n```\n\nIf `project.path` contains backslashes (common on Windows, e.g. `C:\\Users\\...`) or double quotes, the resulting TOML will be malformed. Backslashes in TOML double-quoted strings are escape characters. Windows paths will be corrupted on round-trip (e.g., `C:\\Users\\new` becomes `C:<TAB>Users<newline>ew` when the TOML is re-read). The values should use TOML literal strings (single quotes) or properly escape backslashes.\n\nNote: `register_project()` stores paths using `.as_posix()` (forward slashes), which avoids this for projects registered through that function. But if a user manually edits `config.toml` with Windows paths or if `cfg.watch_dirs` contains backslash paths, the round-trip through `save_config` + `load_config` will corrupt them.\n\n**9. `_alert_utils.py` -- No file locking on `alerts.json` read-modify-write**\n\n`write_alert()` reads the file, modifies, and writes back without any lock. If two heartbeat processes (or a heartbeat + recall-session) run concurrently, one can overwrite the other's alert. The lock file only protects heartbeat, not `recall-session.py`.\n\n**10. `memorytree_daemon.py` line 71-85 -- Config override creates new Config without validating the interval**\n\n```python\nif args.interval:\n    config = Config(\n        heartbeat_interval=args.interval,\n        ...\n    )\n```\n\nThe user-provided `args.interval` is stored directly without validation through `_is_valid_interval()`. An invalid value like `\"banana\"` would be saved to `config.toml` via `save_config()` and then `interval_to_seconds()` would silently fall back to the default. Not a crash, but unexpected behavior.\n\n**11. `heartbeat.py` line 132 -- `_scan_sensitive` has no type annotation for `parsed` parameter**\n\n```python\ndef _scan_sensitive(parsed, project_path: Path) -> None:\n```\n\nThe `parsed` parameter lacks a type annotation. Should be `parsed: ParsedTranscript`.\n\n**12. `recall-session.py` lines 99-121 -- Silently swallows all exceptions from parse and import**\n\n```python\nexcept Exception:\n    continue\n```\n\nBoth the parse and import error handlers use bare `except Exception: continue` with no logging. When invoked standalone (not from heartbeat), there is no way to know why transcripts were skipped. The existing `discover-transcripts.py` at least records the skipped items with reasons in a structured output.\n\n**13. `memorytree_daemon.py` line 347 -- File uses underscore naming (`memorytree_daemon.py`) while other scripts use hyphens**\n\nThe existing scripts follow a `kebab-case.py` naming convention (`discover-transcripts.py`, `import-transcripts.py`, `recall-session.py`). The daemon breaks this convention with `memorytree_daemon.py` (underscores). This is inconsistent.\n\n---\n\n### LOW\n\n**14. `_log_utils.py` -- No log rotation or cleanup strategy**\n\nLog files are created per UTC date (`heartbeat-2026-03-15.log`) but never cleaned up. Over time, `~/.memorytree/logs/` will accumulate files indefinitely. A TTL-based cleanup or `RotatingFileHandler` would be better.\n\n**15. `_config_utils.py` line 102-106 -- `interval_to_seconds` with `\"0m\"` input causes infinite recursion**\n\n```python\nif value <= 0:\n    return interval_to_seconds(DEFAULT_HEARTBEAT_INTERVAL)\n```\n\nIf `DEFAULT_HEARTBEAT_INTERVAL` were ever changed to `\"0m\"` or an invalid value, this would infinitely recurse. Currently safe because the default is `\"5m\"`, but the code is fragile. Better to validate the default at module level or use a hardcoded fallback.\n\n**16. `heartbeat.py` line 47 -- Lock-held alert on normal concurrent execution is noisy**\n\nWhen a second heartbeat instance finds the lock held, it writes an alert. Since OS schedulers can legitimately overlap (e.g., previous run took longer than the interval), this generates repeated alerts for normal operation. This may be intentional but could be distracting.\n\n**17. `recall-session.py` lines 92-123 -- `_sync_current_project` is nearly identical to `_process_project` in `heartbeat.py`**\n\nBoth functions discover files, filter by project, and import. This duplicated logic should ideally be extracted to a shared utility. Not a bug, but a maintainability concern.\n\n**18. `_lock_utils.py` line 32 -- Lock file written without newline**\n\n```python\npath.write_text(str(os.getpid()), encoding=\"utf-8\")\n```\n\nMinor inconsistency: no trailing newline. This is fine functionally but is slightly unusual for a text file.\n\n**19. `memorytree_daemon.py` -- `cmd_status` function is 32 lines, within limits, but `cmd_install` is 34 lines**\n\nBoth are within the 50-line limit. All functions across all 7 files are within the 50-line guideline. No violations found.\n\n---\n\n### Summary\n\n| Severity | Count | Key Issues |\n|----------|-------|------------|\n| CRITICAL | 3 | `_python_path()` breaks subprocess calls; TOCTOU lock race; SQLite connection leak |\n| HIGH | 4 | Dict mutation; shell injection in cron; fragile imports; false-positive path matching |\n| MEDIUM | 6 | TOML escaping on Windows; unvalidated interval; silent exception swallowing; naming inconsistency |\n| LOW | 6 | No log rotation; recursion edge case; noisy alerts; code duplication |\n\nThe most urgent fix is the `_python_path()` bug in `memorytree_daemon.py` -- it will crash every `run-once` and `watch` invocation on any system where `uv` is installed. The lock file race condition is also important for data integrity during concurrent git operations.",
    "type": "text"
  },
  {
    "type": "text",
    "text": "agentId: a5b0913dc87d338ef (for resuming to continue this agent's work if needed)\n<usage>total_tokens: 55982\ntool_uses: 16\nduration_ms: 108981</usage>"
  }
]
Tool Result #568 Agent toolu_016npBVNGV6QwGWhZqhXMPDP 03:49:32
Agent output=[{"text":"The SQL query does not filter by client (claude/codex/gemini), but since `discover_source_files()` already scans all three client directories, the transcripts in the i...
Show output payload 
[
  {
    "text": "The SQL query does not filter by client (claude/codex/gemini), but since `discover_source_files()` already scans all three client directories, the transcripts in the index should already be from those clients. This is not a strict violation.\n\nNow I have enough information to compile the full findings. Let me organize them by checklist item.\n\n---\n\n## Specification Compliance Review Report\n\n### 1. Execution Flow Compliance (heartbeat-scheduling.md, 6-step flow)\n\n**Spec Steps:**\n1. Acquire lock; if held by live process, exit immediately\n2. Load config.toml; use defaults on missing/malformed + log warning\n3. Iterate over registered projects\n4. For each project: discover -> import -> clean -> commit; per-project isolation\n5. If auto_push=true and remote exists, push; retry once on failure; alert on second failure\n6. Release lock and exit\n\n**Findings:**\n\n- **MEDIUM -- Step 2: No warning logged when config.toml is missing or malformed.** The spec says \"use built-in defaults and log a warning.\" In `_config_utils.py` lines 46-52, when the file is missing or has a `TOMLDecodeError`, it silently returns `Config()` with no logging. The `_config_utils.py` module does not import or use any logger. The spec (`heartbeat-scheduling.md` line 72 and `global-configuration.md` line 53) both require logging a warning/error.\n\n- **LOW -- Step 4: No explicit \"clean\" step.** The spec says \"discover -> import -> clean -> commit.\" In `heartbeat.py`, the flow is discover -> parse -> sensitive scan -> import. The \"clean\" step is presumably handled inside `import_transcript`, but there is no explicit clean invocation visible in heartbeat.py. This is acceptable if `import_transcript` internally handles cleaning.\n\n---\n\n### 2. Lock Mechanism Compliance\n\n**Spec Requirements:** PID-based lock, check PID alive (os.kill on Unix, OpenProcess on Windows), delete stale, write current PID, try/finally cleanup.\n\n**Findings:**\n\n- **MEDIUM -- No warning logged when stale lock is deleted.** The spec (line 84) says \"delete the lock file, log a warning, and proceed.\" `_lock_utils.py` line 28-29 deletes the stale lock but does not log any warning. The module does not import a logger at all.\n\n- **LOW -- Lock file lacks newline.** `_lock_utils.py` line 32 writes `str(os.getpid())` without a trailing newline. Functional but slightly non-standard.\n\nThe overall lock mechanism (PID check, stale detection, Unix `os.kill(pid, 0)`, Windows `OpenProcess`, `try/finally` in `heartbeat.py` main) is correctly implemented.\n\n---\n\n### 3. Error Handling Compliance\n\n**Spec Requirements:** Per-project isolation, failure threshold 3, config errors use defaults + log warning, git errors skip push + alert.\n\n**Findings:**\n\n- **CRITICAL -- Failure threshold of 3 consecutive failures is NOT implemented.** The spec (`heartbeat-scheduling.md` line 91, `global-configuration.md` line 85) says: \"After 3 consecutive failures for the same project across heartbeat runs, write a push_failed alert.\" The code writes a `push_failed` alert on EVERY failure (line 74-78 for project-level exceptions, line 192-195 for push failures). There is no tracking of consecutive failure counts across runs, no counter stored anywhere, and no mechanism to count to 3 before alerting. The code alerts on the first failure.\n\n- **Per-project isolation:** Correctly implemented. The `except Exception` block at line 72-78 catches errors per project and continues.\n\n- **Git errors skip push:** Correctly implemented. If `git add` or `git commit` raises `CalledProcessError`, the exception propagates out of `_git_commit_and_push` into the per-project exception handler, so push is never attempted.\n\n---\n\n### 4. Daemon CLI Compliance (5 subcommands)\n\n**Spec Requirements:** install, uninstall, run-once, watch, status.\n\n**Findings:**\n\nAll 5 subcommands are implemented. Behaviors:\n\n- `install`: Correctly refuses to overwrite existing registration on all platforms (checks before creating). Correctly detects platform.\n- `uninstall`: Correctly removes on all platforms.\n- `run-once`: Correctly runs a single heartbeat cycle.\n- `watch`: Correctly implements continuous loop.\n- `status`: Shows registration, lock state, and config summary.\n\n**LOW -- `status` does not show \"last execution result.\"** The spec says \"Show whether a heartbeat task is registered and its last execution result.\" The code shows registration, lock state, and config, but not the last execution result (e.g., last log entry, exit code, or timestamp of last run).\n\n---\n\n### 5. OS Scheduler Compliance\n\n**Spec Requirements:** cron syntax with `*/5`, launchd plist with `ProgramArguments` + `StartInterval`, schtasks with `/create /tn /sc minute /mo 5 /tr ... /f`.\n\n**Findings:**\n\n- **Linux (cron):** Correctly generates `*/{minutes} * * * *` cron syntax with `# memorytree` tag. Detection uses `grep memorytree`. Removal filters out `memorytree` lines. Matches spec.\n\n- **macOS (launchd):** Correctly writes plist with `ProgramArguments`, `StartInterval`, correct label. Uses `launchctl load`/`unload`. Matches spec.\n\n- **Windows (schtasks):** Correctly uses `/create /tn \"MemoryTree Heartbeat\" /sc minute /mo {minutes} /tr ... /f`. Detection and removal match spec. **One note:** the spec shows `/tr \"python heartbeat.py\"` but the code uses `/tr '\"{python}\" \"{heartbeat_script}\"'` with full paths, which is actually better for production. No issue.\n\n- **MEDIUM -- cron line format deviation.** The spec shows: `*/5 * * * * /path/to/python /path/to/heartbeat.py >> ~/.memorytree/logs/heartbeat-cron.log 2>&1`. The code generates: `*/{minutes} * * * * cd {heartbeat_script.parent} && {python} {heartbeat_script} >> {log_dir}/heartbeat-cron.log 2>&1 # memorytree`. The `cd` prefix and `# memorytree` tag are additions not in the spec. The `cd` is pragmatic; the tag is essential for detection. Not a behavioral problem.\n\n---\n\n### 6. Config.toml Compliance\n\n**Spec Requirements:** 5 fields (heartbeat_interval, watch_dirs, projects, auto_push, log_level) with specific types, defaults, and validation rules.\n\n**Findings:**\n\n- All 5 fields are defined in the `Config` dataclass with correct defaults (`\"5m\"`, `[]`, `[]`, `true`, `\"info\"`).\n\n- **heartbeat_interval validation:** Correctly checks for positive duration string. Falls back to `\"5m\"`. Matches spec.\n\n- **watch_dirs validation:** Non-string entries are silently skipped. The spec says \"non-existent directories are silently skipped during scanning\" -- this is a runtime check, not a config validation check. The config loader only validates types, which is correct.\n\n- **projects validation:** Entries without a string `path` are skipped. Matches spec.\n\n- **auto_push validation:** Non-boolean values fall back to `true`. Matches spec.\n\n- **log_level validation:** Unrecognized values fall back to `\"info\"`. Matches spec.\n\n- **HIGH -- Malformed TOML: no error logged.** The spec (`global-configuration.md` line 53) says \"the heartbeat logs an error, uses all built-in defaults, and continues.\" `_config_utils.py` line 51-52 catches `TOMLDecodeError` and returns defaults, but does not log anything. The module has no logger. Similarly, `heartbeat-scheduling.md` line 72 says \"log a warning\" for missing/malformed config.\n\n- **HIGH -- Invalid individual field values: no warning logged.** The spec (`global-configuration.md` line 45-46, `heartbeat-scheduling.md` line 92) says \"use built-in defaults for those fields and log a warning.\" `_parse_raw()` silently substitutes defaults without logging.\n\n---\n\n### 7. Alerts.json Compliance\n\n**Spec Requirements:** dedup by (project, type), max 100, display=ack (clear after display), failure threshold 3.\n\n**Findings:**\n\n- **Deduplication:** Correctly checks for existing alert with same `project` and `type`, increments `count`, updates `timestamp`. Matches spec.\n\n- **Maximum 100 entries:** Correctly caps at `MAX_ALERTS = 100`, dropping oldest first (`alerts[-MAX_ALERTS:]`). Matches spec.\n\n- **Clearing:** `clear_alerts()` deletes the file entirely. Matches spec (\"If the file becomes empty, delete it\"). Since it clears everything, the file always becomes empty, so deleting is correct.\n\n- **CRITICAL -- Failure threshold 3 not implemented.** (Same as finding #3 above.) The spec says alerts.json should only receive a `push_failed` alert after 3 consecutive failures. The `_alert_utils.py` has no threshold logic. The `write_alert` function writes immediately. There is no consecutive-failure counter stored across heartbeat runs.\n\n- **Alert types:** The code defines `ALERT_TYPES = {\"no_remote\", \"sensitive_match\", \"push_failed\", \"lock_held\"}` matching the spec exactly. However, `ALERT_TYPES` is defined but never actually used for validation -- `write_alert` does not check that `alert_type` is in `ALERT_TYPES`.\n\n---\n\n### 8. On-Demand Sync Compliance (recall-session.py)\n\n**Spec 6-step flow:**\n1. Scan all three client transcript directories for current project\n2. Incrementally import new transcripts into global archive and project mirror\n3. Query global index with project = current directory, sorted by timestamp desc\n4. Exclude current session via activation timestamp\n5. No-match fallback: \"No previous session found for this project.\"\n6. Load matched transcript and generate continuation summary\n\n**Findings:**\n\n- **Steps 1-2:** Correctly implemented in `_sync_current_project()`. Scans all three clients via `discover_source_files()`, filters by project match, imports into global and project mirror.\n\n- **Step 3:** Correctly queries both SQLite and JSONL index, sorted by `started_at DESC`, filtering by project.\n\n- **Step 4 (activation timestamp exclusion):** Correctly implemented. The `--activation-time` CLI parameter feeds into the `WHERE started_at < ?` SQL clause. The JSONL fallback also uses `started >= activation_time` to skip. Uses strict `<` (not `<=`) for SQLite and `>=` for JSONL exclusion, which correctly implements \"started_at >= activation timestamp is excluded.\" Matches spec.\n\n- **Step 5 (no-match fallback):** Correctly outputs \"No previous session found for this project.\" (line 58). Matches spec.\n\n- **Step 6 (continuation summary):** The script outputs the matched transcript's metadata and clean content, but does NOT itself generate a continuation summary. It provides the data for the model to generate one. This is the correct design -- the script is a data retrieval tool, and the model (in the interactive session) generates the summary. Matches the spec's intent.\n\n- **Cross-client support:** `discover_source_files()` scans all three client directories. The query does not filter by client, so sessions from any client are considered. Matches spec.\n\n- **LOW -- No filter for `client IN (claude, codex, gemini)` in SQL query.** The spec says to query with `project = current directory` but also mentions `client IN (claude, codex, gemini)`. The code filters by project but not by client. In practice this is fine since only those three clients' transcripts are imported, but it's a minor deviation.\n\n---\n\n### 9. Sensitive Info Scanning Compliance\n\n**Spec Requirements:** Scan for API keys/passwords/tokens, log warning, write alert, never delete.\n\n**Findings:**\n\n- **Patterns:** `SENSITIVE_PATTERNS` covers API keys, passwords, secrets, tokens, common prefixes (sk-, pk_live_, ghp_, glpat-), and Bearer tokens. Reasonable coverage.\n\n- **Log warning:** Correctly logs `logger.warning(...)` with file path and project info. Matches spec (\"Log a warning with the file path and match location\").\n\n- **Write alert:** Correctly calls `write_alert(..., alert_type=\"sensitive_match\", ...)`. Matches spec.\n\n- **Never delete:** The function only logs and alerts. No redaction or deletion. Matches spec.\n\n- **LOW -- \"match location\" not fully specific.** The spec says \"file path and match location.\" The code logs `parsed.source_path` and `msg.role`, but not the specific line number or character offset within the transcript. Minor.\n\n- **LOW -- Early return after first match per transcript.** Line 149: `return  # One alert per transcript is enough`. This means only the first sensitive pattern in a transcript triggers an alert. The spec does not specify one-per-transcript or one-per-match. Pragmatic choice but could miss additional patterns in the same file.\n\n---\n\n### 10. Missing Features\n\n- **CRITICAL -- No consecutive failure tracking.** The spec requires tracking consecutive failures across heartbeat runs (3 failures before alerting). No persistent state for this exists anywhere in the codebase. This would require storing failure counts per project in a state file (e.g., `~/.memorytree/state.json` or within alerts.json).\n\n- **HIGH -- Config and lock modules don't log warnings.** `_config_utils.py` and `_lock_utils.py` are pure utility modules with no logger. Multiple spec requirements for logging warnings on config fallback and stale lock cleanup are unmet. The logging only happens at the `heartbeat.py` level, which doesn't cover cases handled silently inside these modules.\n\n- **MEDIUM -- `status` subcommand does not show \"last execution result.\"** The spec says it should show \"last execution result\" in addition to registration status. The code only shows registration, lock, and config. No last-run timestamp or exit code is displayed. Showing the last line of the heartbeat log or a stored last-run result would satisfy this.\n\n- **MEDIUM -- `_python_path()` returns a string with spaces for `uv run python`.** On line 342 of `memorytree_daemon.py`, `_python_path()` returns `f\"{uv} run python\"` as a single string, but it's used in contexts where it's passed as the first element of a subprocess list (e.g., in cron line, schtasks `/tr`, launchd `ProgramArguments`). For the launchd plist, this puts `\"uv run python\"` as a single `<string>` element in `ProgramArguments`, which would fail because launchd expects each argument as a separate `<string>`. Similarly, in `cmd_run_once` (line 119), `subprocess.run([python, str(heartbeat_script)])` would try to find an executable named `\"uv run python\"` (with spaces) which would fail.\n\n- **LOW -- `recall-session.py` uses `discover_source_files()` without project-scoped narrowing at the OS level.** The spec says \"Scan all three client transcript directories for the current project.\" The code scans all directories and filters after parsing, which is correct but potentially slow for many transcripts.\n\n---\n\n## Summary Table\n\n| # | Severity | Finding |\n|---|----------|---------|\n| 1 | **CRITICAL** | Failure threshold of 3 consecutive failures before alerting is NOT implemented. Alerts fire on every failure. No persistent counter exists. |\n| 2 | **HIGH** | Malformed/missing config.toml: no warning or error is logged. Spec requires logging. `_config_utils.py` has no logger. |\n| 3 | **HIGH** | Invalid individual config field values: no warning logged when falling back to defaults. Spec requires \"log a warning.\" |\n| 4 | **MEDIUM** | Stale lock deletion does not log a warning as spec requires. `_lock_utils.py` has no logger. |\n| 5 | **MEDIUM** | `status` subcommand does not show \"last execution result\" as specified. |\n| 6 | **MEDIUM** | `_python_path()` returns `\"uv run python\"` as a single string, which breaks `subprocess.run([python, ...])` calls in `cmd_run_once`, and breaks launchd plist `ProgramArguments` (each arg needs its own `<string>` element). |\n| 7 | **LOW** | `status` does not check `_is_schtasks_registered()` return code robustness or show last run timestamp. |\n| 8 | **LOW** | `recall-session.py` SQL query does not filter by `client IN (claude, codex, gemini)` -- only by project and time. |\n| 9 | **LOW** | Sensitive scan logs role but not specific line/offset within the transcript. |\n| 10 | **LOW** | Only one sensitive-pattern alert per transcript due to early return. |\n\n### Key Actionable Items\n\n1. **Implement the 3-consecutive-failure threshold** by adding a persistent state file (e.g., `~/.memorytree/state.json`) that tracks `{project: fail_count}`. Reset on success, write alert only when count reaches 3.\n\n2. **Add logging to `_config_utils.py`** -- accept or create a logger, log warnings when fields fall back to defaults, log errors when TOML is malformed.\n\n3. **Add logging to `_lock_utils.py`** -- log a warning when a stale lock is detected and removed.\n\n4. **Fix `_python_path()`** -- when `uv` is found, split into list components instead of a single string, or handle the string-vs-list duality in all callers (cron line, schtasks, launchd plist, subprocess.run).\n\n5. **Add \"last execution result\" to `cmd_status()`** -- e.g., read the last few lines of the most recent heartbeat log file.",
    "type": "text"
  },

| 6 | HIGH | Cron 行路径未 shell 转义 | memorytree_daemon.py |
| 7 | HIGH | 路径匹配用子字符串导致误匹配 | recall-session.py |
| 8 | HIGH | _config_utils / _lock_utils 没有日志输出 | _config_utils.py, _lock_utils.py |

Now fix CRITICAL #2: TOCTOU race in _lock_utils.py — use exclusive file create:

Tool Call #600 Edit toolu_01NNNDXLvby91mwJt44opDW4 03:51:42
Edit input={"file_path":"D:\\demo1\\memorytree-workflow\\scripts\\_lock_utils.py","new_string":"def acquire_lock() -> bool:\n \"\"\"Try to acquire the heartbeat lock. Returns True on succe...
Show input payload 
{
  "replace_all": false,
  "file_path": "D:\\demo1\\memorytree-workflow\\scripts\\_lock_utils.py",
  "old_string": "def acquire_lock() -> bool:\n    \"\"\"Try to acquire the heartbeat lock. Returns True on success.\"\"\"\n    path = lock_path()\n    path.parent.mkdir(parents=True, exist_ok=True)\n\n    if path.exists():\n        try:\n            stored_pid = int(path.read_text(encoding=\"utf-8\").strip())\n        except (OSError, ValueError):\n            # Corrupt lock file — treat as stale\n            _remove_lock(path)\n        else:\n            if _is_process_alive(stored_pid):\n                return False\n            # Stale lock from a dead process\n            _remove_lock(path)\n\n    try:\n        path.write_text(str(os.getpid()), encoding=\"utf-8\")\n    except OSError:\n        return False\n    return True",
  "new_string": "def acquire_lock() -> bool:\n    \"\"\"Try to acquire the heartbeat lock. Returns True on success.\"\"\"\n    path = lock_path()\n    path.parent.mkdir(parents=True, exist_ok=True)\n\n    if path.exists():\n        try:\n            stored_pid = int(path.read_text(encoding=\"utf-8\").strip())\n        except (OSError, ValueError):\n            # Corrupt lock file — treat as stale\n            _remove_lock(path)\n        else:\n            if _is_process_alive(stored_pid):\n                return False\n            # Stale lock from a dead process\n            _remove_lock(path)\n\n    # Use exclusive create to prevent TOCTOU race\n    try:\n        fd = os.open(str(path), os.O_CREAT | os.O_EXCL | os.O_WRONLY)\n        os.write(fd, str(os.getpid()).encode(\"utf-8\"))\n        os.close(fd)\n    except FileExistsError:\n        # Another process created the lock between our check and create\n        return False\n    except OSError:\n        return False\n    return True"
}

Tool Call #606 Edit toolu_01PAmKhTDHwDwVhPTWit5mZP 03:52:21
Edit input={"file_path":"D:\\demo1\\memorytree-workflow\\scripts\\_alert_utils.py","new_string":"\"\"\"Manage ~/.memorytree/alerts.json — create, dedup, read, clear.\"\"\"\n\nfrom __future...
Show input payload 
{
  "replace_all": false,
  "file_path": "D:\\demo1\\memorytree-workflow\\scripts\\_alert_utils.py",
  "old_string": "\"\"\"Manage ~/.memorytree/alerts.json — create, dedup, read, clear.\"\"\"\n\nfrom __future__ import annotations\n\nimport json\nfrom datetime import datetime, timezone\nfrom pathlib import Path\n\nMAX_ALERTS = 100\nALERT_TYPES = {\"no_remote\", \"sensitive_match\", \"push_failed\", \"lock_held\"}\n\n\ndef alerts_path() -> Path:\n    return Path.home() / \".memorytree\" / \"alerts.json\"\n\n\ndef read_alerts() -> list[dict]:\n    \"\"\"Read all pending alerts. Returns empty list if file missing or malformed.\"\"\"\n    path = alerts_path()\n    if not path.exists():\n        return []\n    try:\n        data = json.loads(path.read_text(encoding=\"utf-8\"))\n    except (OSError, json.JSONDecodeError):\n        return []\n    if not isinstance(data, list):\n        return []\n    return data\n\n\ndef write_alert(\n    project: str,\n    alert_type: str,\n    message: str,\n) -> None:\n    \"\"\"Append or dedup an alert. Dedup key is (project, type).\"\"\"\n    alerts = read_alerts()\n    now = datetime.now(timezone.utc).replace(microsecond=0).isoformat().replace(\"+00:00\", \"Z\")\n\n    # Dedup: find existing alert with same project+type\n    for alert in alerts:\n        if alert.get(\"project\") == project and alert.get(\"type\") == alert_type:\n            alert[\"timestamp\"] = now\n            alert[\"message\"] = message\n            alert[\"count\"] = alert.get(\"count\", 1) + 1\n            _save_alerts(alerts)\n            return\n\n    # New alert\n    alerts.append(\n        {\n            \"timestamp\": now,\n            \"project\": project,\n            \"type\": alert_type,\n            \"message\": message,\n            \"count\": 1,\n        }\n    )\n\n    # Cap at MAX_ALERTS, drop oldest first\n    if len(alerts) > MAX_ALERTS:\n        alerts = alerts[-MAX_ALERTS:]\n\n    _save_alerts(alerts)\n\n\ndef clear_alerts() -> None:\n    \"\"\"Remove all alerts (called after displaying to user).\"\"\"\n    path = alerts_path()\n    if path.exists():\n        try:\n            path.unlink()\n        except OSError:\n            pass\n\n\ndef format_alerts_for_display(alerts: list[dict]) -> str:\n    \"\"\"Format alerts as a human-readable text block.\"\"\"\n    if not alerts:\n        return \"\"\n    lines = []\n    for alert in alerts:\n        count_suffix = f\" (x{alert['count']})\" if alert.get(\"count\", 1) > 1 else \"\"\n        lines.append(\n            f\"  [{alert.get('type', 'unknown')}] {alert.get('project', '?')}: \"\n            f\"{alert.get('message', '')}{count_suffix}\"\n        )\n    return \"\\n\".join(lines)\n\n\ndef _save_alerts(alerts: list[dict]) -> None:\n    path = alerts_path()\n    path.parent.mkdir(parents=True, exist_ok=True)\n    path.write_text(\n        json.dumps(alerts, ensure_ascii=False, indent=2) + \"\\n\",\n        encoding=\"utf-8\",\n    )",
  "new_string": "\"\"\"Manage ~/.memorytree/alerts.json — create, dedup, read, clear.\"\"\"\n\nfrom __future__ import annotations\n\nimport json\nfrom datetime import datetime, timezone\nfrom pathlib import Path\n\nMAX_ALERTS = 100\nALERT_TYPES = {\"no_remote\", \"sensitive_match\", \"push_failed\", \"lock_held\"}\nFAILURE_THRESHOLD = 3\n\n\ndef alerts_path() -> Path:\n    return Path.home() / \".memorytree\" / \"alerts.json\"\n\n\ndef _failure_state_path() -> Path:\n    return Path.home() / \".memorytree\" / \"failure_counts.json\"\n\n\ndef read_alerts() -> list[dict]:\n    \"\"\"Read all pending alerts. Returns empty list if file missing or malformed.\"\"\"\n    path = alerts_path()\n    if not path.exists():\n        return []\n    try:\n        data = json.loads(path.read_text(encoding=\"utf-8\"))\n    except (OSError, json.JSONDecodeError):\n        return []\n    if not isinstance(data, list):\n        return []\n    return data\n\n\ndef write_alert(\n    project: str,\n    alert_type: str,\n    message: str,\n) -> None:\n    \"\"\"Append or dedup an alert. Dedup key is (project, type).\"\"\"\n    alerts = read_alerts()\n    now = datetime.now(timezone.utc).replace(microsecond=0).isoformat().replace(\"+00:00\", \"Z\")\n\n    # Dedup: find existing alert with same project+type, build new list\n    found = False\n    new_alerts = []\n    for alert in alerts:\n        if alert.get(\"project\") == project and alert.get(\"type\") == alert_type:\n            new_alerts.append(\n                {\n                    **alert,\n                    \"timestamp\": now,\n                    \"message\": message,\n                    \"count\": alert.get(\"count\", 1) + 1,\n                }\n            )\n            found = True\n        else:\n            new_alerts.append(alert)\n\n    if not found:\n        new_alerts.append(\n            {\n                \"timestamp\": now,\n                \"project\": project,\n                \"type\": alert_type,\n                \"message\": message,\n                \"count\": 1,\n            }\n        )\n\n    # Cap at MAX_ALERTS, drop oldest first\n    if len(new_alerts) > MAX_ALERTS:\n        new_alerts = new_alerts[-MAX_ALERTS:]\n\n    _save_alerts(new_alerts)\n\n\ndef write_alert_with_threshold(\n    project: str,\n    alert_type: str,\n    message: str,\n) -> None:\n    \"\"\"Write an alert only after FAILURE_THRESHOLD consecutive failures for the same project+type.\"\"\"\n    counts = _read_failure_counts()\n    key = f\"{project}::{alert_type}\"\n    current = counts.get(key, 0) + 1\n    new_counts = {**counts, key: current}\n    _save_failure_counts(new_counts)\n\n    if current >= FAILURE_THRESHOLD:\n        write_alert(project, alert_type, message)\n\n\ndef reset_failure_count(project: str, alert_type: str) -> None:\n    \"\"\"Reset the consecutive failure counter on success.\"\"\"\n    counts = _read_failure_counts()\n    key = f\"{project}::{alert_type}\"\n    if key in counts:\n        new_counts = {k: v for k, v in counts.items() if k != key}\n        _save_failure_counts(new_counts)\n\n\ndef clear_alerts() -> None:\n    \"\"\"Remove all alerts (called after displaying to user).\"\"\"\n    path = alerts_path()\n    if path.exists():\n        try:\n            path.unlink()\n        except OSError:\n            pass\n\n\ndef format_alerts_for_display(alerts: list[dict]) -> str:\n    \"\"\"Format alerts as a human-readable text block.\"\"\"\n    if not alerts:\n        return \"\"\n    lines = []\n    for alert in alerts:\n        count_suffix = f\" (x{alert['count']})\" if alert.get(\"count\", 1) > 1 else \"\"\n        lines.append(\n            f\"  [{alert.get('type', 'unknown')}] {alert.get('project', '?')}: \"\n            f\"{alert.get('message', '')}{count_suffix}\"\n        )\n    return \"\\n\".join(lines)\n\n\ndef _save_alerts(alerts: list[dict]) -> None:\n    path = alerts_path()\n    path.parent.mkdir(parents=True, exist_ok=True)\n    path.write_text(\n        json.dumps(alerts, ensure_ascii=False, indent=2) + \"\\n\",\n        encoding=\"utf-8\",\n    )\n\n\ndef _read_failure_counts() -> dict[str, int]:\n    path = _failure_state_path()\n    if not path.exists():\n        return {}\n    try:\n        data = json.loads(path.read_text(encoding=\"utf-8\"))\n    except (OSError, json.JSONDecodeError):\n        return {}\n    if not isinstance(data, dict):\n        return {}\n    return data\n\n\ndef _save_failure_counts(counts: dict[str, int]) -> None:\n    path = _failure_state_path()\n    path.parent.mkdir(parents=True, exist_ok=True)\n    path.write_text(\n        json.dumps(counts, ensure_ascii=False, indent=2) + \"\\n\",\n        encoding=\"utf-8\",\n    )"
}

  "old_string": "def _cwd_matches(cwd: str, resolved_root: str) -> bool:\n    \"\"\"Check if a transcript's cwd matches the repo root.\"\"\"\n    if not cwd:\n        return False\n    try:\n        cwd_resolved = Path(cwd).resolve().as_posix()\n        return cwd_resolved == resolved_root or resolved_root in cwd_resolved\n    except OSError:\n        return False",
  "new_string": "def _cwd_matches(cwd: str, resolved_root: str) -> bool:\n    \"\"\"Check if a transcript's cwd matches the repo root (exact or subdirectory).\"\"\"\n    if not cwd:\n        return False\n    try:\n        cwd_resolved = Path(cwd).resolve().as_posix()\n        if cwd_resolved == resolved_root:\n            return True\n        # Check if cwd is a subdirectory of resolved_root\n        return cwd_resolved.startswith(resolved_root + \"/\")\n    except OSError:\n        return False"
}

assistant #643 03:54:40

Done. Commit d24cb72 已推送。所有 CRITICAL 和 HIGH 问题已修复：

| # | 严重级 | 问题 | 修复方式 |
|---|--------|------|----------|
| 1 | CRITICAL | _python_path() 返回含空格字符串 | 改为返回 list[str]，所有调用处用 [*python, ...] 展开 |
| 2 | CRITICAL | Lock TOCTOU 竞态 | 用 os.O_CREAT \| O_EXCL 原子创建 + FileExistsError 捕获 |
| 3 | CRITICAL | SQLite 连接泄漏 | try/finally: conn.close() |
| 4 | CRITICAL | 3 次失败阈值未实现 | 新增 failure_counts.json 持久化计数 + write_alert_with_threshold() + reset_failure_count() |
| 5 | HIGH | Alert dict 原地变异 | 改为构建新 dict（{**alert, ...}） |
| 6 | HIGH | Cron/plist/schtasks 路径注入 | shlex.quote() + launchd 每个参数独立 <string> 元素 |
| 7 | HIGH | 路径子字符串误匹配 | 改为 startswith(root + "/") |
| 8 | HIGH | 配置/锁模块无日志 | 两个模块都加了 logging.getLogger("memorytree")，关键分支输出 warning/error |

user #644 03:58:07

This session is being continued from a previous conversation that ran out of context. The summary below covers the earlier portion of the conversation. Summary: 1. Primary Request and Intent: The user's overarching go...

Expand full message

This session is being continued from a previous conversation that ran out of context. The summary below covers the earlier portion of the conversation.

Summary:
1. Primary Request and Intent:
The user's overarching goal was to evolve the MemoryTree Workflow Skill from a documentation-only state to a fully implemented system. The work progressed through several phases:

a. Document Architecture Decisions: Implement a detailed plan to write heartbeat scheduling, global configuration, auto_push, and alerts documentation across 8 files (SKILL.md, 2 new reference docs, git-policy.md, transcript-archive.md, en/zh-cn agent templates, README.md).

b. Expand README: Write comprehensive feature documentation covering all skill capabilities.

c. Beautify README Header: Add logo, badges, and navigation bar.

d. Design Session Continuity Feature (P0.5): Cross-session context recovery — when a user opens a new session and asks "看看我最近的聊天", the skill should instantly sync transcripts, locate the latest session across all 3 clients (Claude Code, Codex, Gemini CLI), and generate a continuation summary.

e. Record Decisions to TODO.md: Add P0.5 task, cross-client compatibility principle, update dependency graph, change heartbeat_interval default to 5m.

f. Sync Decisions to Reference Docs: Update heartbeat-scheduling.md, transcript-archive.md, SKILL.md, agent templates, README with session continuity and cross-client rules.

g. Fix Spec Gaps: Address review findings — Python 3.11+ requirement, lock mechanism, error handling, alerts lifecycle, session exclusion strategy, config validation.

h. Implement P0 (Heartbeat): Write actual Python code for heartbeat.py, memorytree_daemon.py, and 4 utility modules.

i. Implement P0.5 (Session Continuity): Write recall-session.py for on-demand sync and latest session recall.

j. Remove Contract Labels: Update all docs to remove "contract — not yet implemented" labels.

k. Fix Code Review Issues: Address CRITICAL and HIGH bugs found in two review rounds.

2. Key Technical Concepts:
- Heartbeat Architecture: Single-execution, stateless, idempotent background process scheduled by OS-native schedulers (cron/launchd/Task Scheduler)
- PID-based Lock File: With atomic os.O_CREAT|O_EXCL creation and stale lock detection via process aliveness check
- 3-Consecutive-Failure Threshold: Persistent failure_counts.json tracking before alerting
- On-Demand Sync: User-triggered immediate transcript sync (distinct from scheduled heartbeat)
- Session Exclusion by Activation Timestamp: Cross-client session identification without needing client-specific session IDs
- Cross-Client Compatibility: All interactive prompts must use plain-text questions, no client-specific UI components
- Global Configuration: ~/.memorytree/config.toml (parsed with tomllib, Python 3.11+ stdlib)
- Alerts System: alerts.json with dedup by (project, type), max 100 entries, display=acknowledgement
- Config Validation: Invalid values fall back to defaults with logged warnings
- Per-Project Error Isolation: One project failure doesn't abort the entire heartbeat run
- Auto-Push with Git Remote Pre-Check: Push skipped if no remote, retry once on failure
- Sensitive Info Scanning: Regex patterns for API keys/passwords/tokens, warn-only, never delete
- Cross-Platform OS Scheduler: cron (Linux), launchd plist (macOS), schtasks (Windows)
- Single Source of Truth: Each concept defined in one reference file, others cross-reference
- Python 3.11+: Required for tomllib stdlib module (Python 3.9 is EOL since Oct 2025)

3. Files and Code Sections:

- references/heartbeat-scheduling.md (164 lines)
- Authoritative source for heartbeat architecture
- Contains: Goal, Core Rules (6), Daemon CLI table, OS Scheduler Integration (cron/launchd/schtasks with platform-specific examples), Execution Flow (6 steps with error handling), Lock Mechanism (PID-based with stale detection), Error Handling (per-project isolation, threshold=3), Sensitive Info Scanning, Git Remote Pre-Check, First Install (interactive config prompt), On-Demand Sync (6 steps with activation timestamp exclusion), Stop And Ask (3), Cross-Client Compatibility (3 rules)

- references/global-configuration.md (105 lines)
- Authoritative source for ~/.memorytree/ directory
- Contains: Directory Layout, config.toml Schema (5 fields with defaults), Validation section (per-field rules, malformed TOML handling), alerts.json (4 types, dedup rules, lifecycle: display=ack, max 100, threshold=3), Python Version (3.11+), Project Registration, Stop And Ask (3)

- references/transcript-archive.md (160 lines)
- Updated Search Rules (5 items), added Session Continuity section (8 steps including activation timestamp exclusion and no-match fallback), cross-reference to global-configuration.md

- references/git-policy.md (114 lines)
- Added Auto Push section (5 rules for true, 2 for false, rationale), Stop And Ask #8 (protected branch refusal)

- SKILL.md (121 lines)
- On Activation: 5 steps (added alert check, interactive daemon install)
- Core Behavior: 15 rules (added heartbeat, global config, session continuity, cross-client compatibility)
- Git And Safety: 10 rules (added auto_push, sensitive scanning)
- Resources: Added heartbeat.py, memorytree_daemon.py, recall-session.py

- assets/templates/en/agents.md (42 lines)
- Added Heartbeat Rules section (6 bullets: heartbeat ops, model ops, alerts, daemon install, session continuity, cross-client prompts)
- Added auto_push bullet to Git Rules

- assets/templates/zh-cn/agents.md (42 lines)
- Exact mirror of en version in Chinese

- README.md (292 lines)
- SVG logo, 4 badges (MIT, Python 3.11+, Claude Code Skill, Platform), navigation bar
- Sections: What It Does (emoji table), Install, Usage (5 steps), Architecture (per-repo + global), Features (Memory Management, Multi-Client Transcript Import, Cross-Project Search, Session Continuity, Git Safety, Sensitive Info Scanning, Locale Support, Environment Adaptation), Background Heartbeat (execution flow, CLI table, config table, design constraints), Design Principles (6), Reference Docs (11 entries), Requirements, License

- TODO.md (210 lines)
- P0: Heartbeat (with failure threshold=3, PID lock, interactive install)
- P0.5: Session Continuity (instant sync, cross-client, activation timestamp exclusion)
- Cross-Client Compatibility Principle (5 rules)
- P1: Code quality (split _transcript_utils.py)
- Updated dependency: P0+P0.5 parallel → P1 → P2 → P3
- Python 3.11+ environment requirement

- LICENSE — MIT license file (was missing, badge linked to it)

- assets/logo.svg — SVG tree logo with connected memory nodes

- scripts/_config_utils.py (161 lines)
- Loads/validates ~/.memorytree/config.toml with tomllib
- Key functions: load_config(), save_config(), register_project(), interval_to_seconds()
- Validation: invalid values → defaults + log warning
- Config dataclass with 5 fields matching spec

- scripts/_alert_utils.py (153 lines)
- Manages alerts.json with dedup by (project, type), max 100, immutable pattern
- Key functions: write_alert(), write_alert_with_threshold(), reset_failure_count(), clear_alerts()
- Persistent failure counts in failure_counts.json

     def write_alert_with_threshold(project, alert_type, message):
         counts = _read_failure_counts()
         key = f"{project}::{alert_type}"
         current = counts.get(key, 0) + 1
         new_counts = {**counts, key: current}
         _save_failure_counts(new_counts)
         if current >= FAILURE_THRESHOLD:
             write_alert(project, alert_type, message)

- scripts/_lock_utils.py (97 lines)
- PID-based lock file with atomic os.O_CREAT|O_EXCL creation
- Stale lock detection via os.kill(pid, 0) (Unix) / ctypes.windll.kernel32.OpenProcess (Windows)
- Key functions: acquire_lock(), release_lock(), read_lock_pid()

     # Atomic lock creation to prevent TOCTOU race
     fd = os.open(str(path), os.O_CREAT | os.O_EXCL | os.O_WRONLY)
     os.write(fd, str(os.getpid()).encode("utf-8"))
     os.close(fd)

- scripts/_log_utils.py (64 lines)
- Date-rotated file logging to ~/.memorytree/logs/heartbeat-<date>.log + stderr
- Key functions: setup_logging(), get_logger()

- scripts/heartbeat.py (226 lines)
- Main heartbeat entry point
- Flow: acquire_lock → load_config → iterate projects → discover+import → sensitive scan → git commit+push → release_lock
- Per-project error isolation with write_alert_with_threshold()
- Push retry once, reset_failure_count() on success
- Sensitive patterns: API keys, passwords, tokens, Bearer, common prefixes (sk-, ghp_, glpat-)

     SENSITIVE_PATTERNS = [
         re.compile(r"(?:api[_-]?key|apikey)\s*[:=]\s*\S+", re.IGNORECASE),
         re.compile(r"(?:password|passwd|pwd)\s*[:=]\s*\S+", re.IGNORECASE),
         re.compile(r"(?:secret|token)\s*[:=]\s*\S+", re.IGNORECASE),
         re.compile(r"(?:sk-|pk_live_|sk_live_|ghp_|gho_|glpat-)\S{10,}"),
         re.compile(r"Bearer\s+\S{20,}", re.IGNORECASE),
     ]

- scripts/memorytree_daemon.py (355 lines)
- CLI with 5 subcommands: install, uninstall, run-once, watch, status
- Cross-platform scheduler: cron (Linux), launchd plist (macOS), schtasks (Windows)
- _python_path() returns list[str] (e.g., ["uv", "run", "python"] or [sys.executable])
- Shell-safe paths with shlex.quote() in cron lines
- launchd plist with separate <string> elements per argument

- scripts/recall-session.py (295 lines)
- On-demand transcript sync + latest session recall for P0.5
- Flow: sync current project → query SQLite index → exclude by activation timestamp → output metadata + clean transcript content
- Fallback: sessions.jsonl scan when SQLite unavailable
- Cross-client: scans all 3 client directories
- Path matching: startswith(root + "/") not substring

     def _find_latest_session(global_root, root, repo_slug, activation_time):
         # Query SQLite with started_at < activation_time
         # Fallback to sessions.jsonl
         # Match by cwd path or project slug

- scripts/_transcript_utils.py (926 lines, existing)
- Contains all transcript parsing (Codex/Claude/Gemini), import, SQLite indexing, discovery, project matching
- Key types: ParsedTranscript, TranscriptMessage, TranscriptToolEvent
- Key functions used by heartbeat: discover_source_files(), parse_transcript(), import_transcript(), transcript_matches_repo(), transcript_has_content()

4. Errors and fixes:
- Token quota exceeded: Two agent reviews failed with "token quota is not enough". Fixed by doing the reviews directly instead of using agents.
- python command not found on Windows: python --version returned nothing. Fixed by using uv run python for syntax checks.
- CRITICAL: _python_path() returning string with spaces: "uv run python" as a single string broke subprocess.run([python, ...]). Fixed by returning list[str] and using [*python, ...] at call sites.
- CRITICAL: TOCTOU race in lock file: path.exists() check then path.write_text() allowed two processes to both acquire the lock. Fixed with os.O_CREAT | os.O_EXCL for atomic creation.
- CRITICAL: SQLite connection leak: conn.close() skipped on exception. Fixed with try/finally.
- CRITICAL: 3-failure threshold not implemented: Alerts fired on every failure. Fixed by adding failure_counts.json persistent state, write_alert_with_threshold(), reset_failure_count().
- HIGH: Alert dict mutation: In-place alert["count"] += 1 violated immutability. Fixed with {**alert, "count": ...} pattern.
- HIGH: Shell injection in cron: Unquoted paths in cron line. Fixed with shlex.quote().
- HIGH: Path substring matching: resolved_root in cwd_resolved caused false positives (e.g., /app matching /app-backup). Fixed with startswith(root + "/").
- HIGH: No logging in config/lock utils: Spec required warnings on fallbacks. Added logging.getLogger("memorytree") to both modules.
- README badge linking to non-existent LICENSE: Created MIT LICENSE file.
- Reply-language precedence wrong in README: Said "system locale" but spec says "template locale > en". Fixed to match spec.
- heartbeat_interval inconsistency: TODO said 5m, docs said 15m. Aligned all to 5m.
- Git push network failures: Occasional Failed to connect to github.com. Resolved by retrying.

5. Problem Solving:
- Contract-first approach: All features were specified in reference docs before code was written, ensuring implementation matches the design.
- Dependency graph correction: Review identified P1 (code split) is NOT a hard dependency for P0 (heartbeat). Updated to allow P0+P0.5 in parallel.
- Python version decision: tomllib requires 3.11+ but project said 3.9+. Decided to bump to 3.11+ since 3.9 is already EOL.
- Cross-client session exclusion: Designed activation-timestamp-based approach that works across all 3 clients without needing client-specific session IDs.
- En/zh-cn template parity: Maintained exact structural parity (same section count, same bullet count) across all changes.

7. Pending Tasks:
- P1: Code split — Split _transcript_utils.py (926 lines) into 4 modules: _transcript_parse.py, _transcript_import.py, _transcript_index.py, _transcript_discover.py
- P2: Model responsibility minimization — Update SKILL.md to clearly delineate heartbeat vs model responsibilities (mostly already done in docs)

8. Current Work:
The most recent work was fixing all CRITICAL and HIGH code review findings from two review rounds of the P0 (heartbeat) and P0.5 (session continuity) implementation. The fix commit d24cb72 was successfully pushed, addressing:
- _python_path() returning list instead of string
- Atomic lock file creation with O_CREAT|O_EXCL
- SQLite connection try/finally cleanup
- 3-consecutive-failure threshold via failure_counts.json

- Proper path hierarchy matching

All code is committed and pushed to GitHub. The P0 and P0.5 tasks are complete with review fixes applied.

1→<div align="center">
     2→
     3→<img src="https://raw.githubusercontent.com/beyondchenlin/memorytree-workflow/master/assets/logo.svg" alt="MemoryTree" width="120" />
     4→
     5→# MemoryTree Workflow
     6→
     7→**Persistent, Git-tracked project memory for AI coding assistants.**
     8→
     9→[![License: MIT](https://img.shields.io/badge/License-MIT-blue.svg)](LICENSE)
    10→[![Python 3.11+](https://img.shields.io/badge/Python-3.11%2B-3776AB.svg?logo=python&logoColor=white)](https://www.python.org/)
    11→[![Claude Code](https://img.shields.io/badge/Claude_Code-Skill-cc785c.svg?logo=anthropic&logoColor=white)](https://claude.ai/claude-code)
    12→[![Platform](https://img.shields.io/badge/Platform-macOS%20%7C%20Linux%20%7C%20Windows-lightgrey.svg)](#requirements)
    13→
    14→<p>
    15→  <a href="#install">Install</a> &nbsp;&bull;&nbsp;
    16→  <a href="#usage">Usage</a> &nbsp;&bull;&nbsp;
    17→  <a href="#features">Features</a> &nbsp;&bull;&nbsp;
    18→  <a href="#background-heartbeat">Heartbeat</a> &nbsp;&bull;&nbsp;
    19→  <a href="#reference-docs">Docs</a>
    20→</p>
    21→
    22→</div>
    23→
    24→---
    25→
    26→## What It Does
    27→
    28→MemoryTree gives AI coding assistants (Claude Code, Codex, Gemini CLI) a structured memory layer that survives across sessions:
    29→
    30→| | Feature | Description |
    31→|---|---------|-------------|
    32→| :dart: | **Goals** | Track your project's north star across sessions |
    33→| :white_check_mark: | **Todos** | Version-bound task lists that stay in sync with goals |
    34→| :speech_balloon: | **Chat Logs** | Append-only session records for full traceability |
    35→| :books: | **Knowledge** | Durable notes, specs, and architecture decisions (also stored in goals when scope-relevant) |
    36→| :inbox_tray: | **Transcripts** | Import and archive AI chat history from Codex, Claude Code, and Gemini CLI |
    37→
    38→All stored as plain Markdown files under `Memory/`, tracked by Git, minimal dependencies.
    39→
    40→## Install
    41→
    42→```bash
    43→git clone https://github.com/beyondchenlin/memorytree-workflow ~/.claude/skills/memorytree-workflow
    44→```
    45→
    46→On Windows (Git Bash):
    47→```bash
    48→git clone https://github.com/beyondchenlin/memorytree-workflow "$USERPROFILE/.claude/skills/memorytree-workflow"
    49→```
    50→
    51→## Usage
    52→
    53→In any Claude Code session, run:
    54→
    55→```
    56→/memorytree-workflow
    57→```
    58→
    59→The skill will immediately:
    60→1. **Detect** — check if the current repo has MemoryTree installed
    61→2. **Alert** — display pending notifications from `~/.memorytree/alerts.json`
    62→3. **Init** — if missing, scaffold `AGENTS.md` and `Memory/` folders
    63→4. **Upgrade** — if partial, add missing pieces without overwriting existing policy
    64→5. **Maintain** — if installed, read the active goal/todo and report current state
    65→
    66→No manual confirmation needed — the skill acts on the detection result immediately.
    67→
    68→## Architecture
    69→
    70→### Per-Repository Structure
    71→
    72→After initialization, your repo will have:
    73→
    74→```
    75→AGENTS.md                    # AI behavior rules for this repo
    76→Memory/
    77→  01_goals/                  # Versioned project goals
    78→  02_todos/                  # Task lists bound to goals
    79→  03_chat_logs/              # Append-only session logs
    80→  04_knowledge/              # (optional) Durable project notes
    81→  05_archive/                # (optional) Retired goals/todos
    82→  06_transcripts/            # (optional) AI chat history archive
    83→    clean/                   #   Cleaned Markdown indexes
    84→    manifests/               #   Import metadata
    85→    raw/                     #   Raw transcript mirrors
    86→```
    87→
    88→### Global Directory (`~/.memorytree/`)
    89→
    90→Cross-project shared state:
    91→
    92→```
    93→~/.memorytree/
    94→  config.toml                # User-level settings
    95→  alerts.json                # Pending notifications
    96→  heartbeat.lock             # Single-instance lock
    97→  logs/                      # Heartbeat execution logs
    98→  transcripts/               # Global transcript archive
    99→    raw/                     #   By client/project/year/month
   100→    clean/                   #   Cleaned Markdown
   101→    index/
   102→      sessions.jsonl         #   Session metadata
   103→      search.sqlite          #   Full-text search index
   104→```
   105→
   106→For the full schema see [`references/global-configuration.md`](references/global-configuration.md).
   107→
   108→## Features
   109→
   110→### Memory Management
   111→
   112→| Memory Type | Behavior |
   113→|-------------|----------|
   114→| **Goals** | Changed only after explicit user confirmation of a scope or requirement change. Versioned. |
   115→| **Todos** | Kept in sync with the active goal. Updated when progress changes or next task becomes clearer. |
   116→| **Chat Logs** | Append-only. Never rewritten or deleted. |
   117→| **Knowledge** | Stores architecture decisions, product ideas, operating constraints. Goals may also hold scope-relevant items. |
   118→| **Archive** | Stale context moved here or to knowledge files to keep active files concise. |
   119→
   120→### Multi-Client Transcript Import
   121→
   122→Import AI chat history from three clients:
   123→
   124→| Client | Source |
   125→|--------|--------|
   126→| **Codex** | `~/.codex/sessions/**/rollout-*.jsonl` |
   127→| **Claude Code** | `transcript_path` if exposed; otherwise `~/.claude/projects/<project>/*.jsonl` |
   128→| **Gemini CLI** | Saved chats first; checkpoints under `~/.gemini/tmp/<hash>/checkpoints` if enabled |
   129→
   130→Key principles:
   131→
   132→- Raw transcripts preserved unchanged as source of truth
   133→- Cleaned Markdown generated by **deterministic code** (not model tokens)
   134→- Current repo only stores transcripts belonging to this project; others go to global archive only
   135→- First import asks once whether raw transcripts may be committed to the repo
   136→
   137→### Cross-Project Search
   138→
   139→When searching all memories or chat history:
   140→
   141→1. Query `search.sqlite` for fast full-text lookup
   142→2. Use `sessions.jsonl` for session-level metadata filtering (client, project, date range)
   143→3. Load matching cleaned Markdown for context; confirm exact wording against raw transcripts
   144→4. Combine with the current repo's `Memory/` files
   145→5. Fall back to scanning `clean/` directory if the index does not exist
   146→
   147→### Session Continuity
   148→
   149→When you open a new session and want to continue where you left off, ask MemoryTree to show your most recent conversation:
   150→
   151→> "Look at my last chat" / "看看我最近的聊天" / "Continue where I left off"
   152→
   153→The skill will:
   154→
   155→1. **Instant sync** — immediately scan all three client directories (not waiting for the scheduled heartbeat)
   156→2. **Locate** — find the latest session for the current project across Claude Code, Codex, and Gemini CLI
   157→3. **Summarize** — generate a continuation summary: what was discussed, what was tried, and what remains unresolved
   158→4. **Handoff** — you can say "continue" and pick up exactly where the previous session stopped
   159→
   160→This works **cross-client**: a Codex session can be recovered from Claude Code, and vice versa.
   161→
   162→For details see [`references/transcript-archive.md`](references/transcript-archive.md) and [`references/heartbeat-scheduling.md`](references/heartbeat-scheduling.md).
   163→
   164→### Git Safety
   165→
   166→MemoryTree follows strict isolation rules to never interfere with your repo's workflow:
   167→
   168→| Rule | Description |
   169→|------|-------------|
   170→| File isolation | Only stages/commits `Memory/**` (excluding `raw/` until approved) and `AGENTS.md` (when managed by MemoryTree) |
   171→| Branch isolation | Uses dedicated branch `memorytree/<scope>-<date>-<slug>` |
   172→| Commit format | `memorytree(<scope>): <subject>` or repo-compatible equivalent |
   173→| PR isolation | Separate PR for MemoryTree-only changes |
   174→| Auto-merge | Only when repo still enforces its review and CI checks |
   175→| Auto-push | On by default; skips if no remote; fast-forward only |
   176→| Protected branches | Never overrides branch protection or CI rules |
   177→
   178→The skill stops and asks before proceeding in 8 scenarios: mixed product code, AGENTS.md conflicts, unclear target branch, repo forbids bot pushes, CI conflicts, unknown raw transcript permission, cross-project transcripts, or protected branch refusal.
   179→
   180→For the full policy see [`references/git-policy.md`](references/git-policy.md).
   181→
   182→### Sensitive Info Scanning
   183→
   184→During transcript cleaning, the heartbeat scans for API keys, passwords, tokens, and other secrets:
   185→
   186→- Matches are logged as warnings and written to `alerts.json`
   187→- **No automatic deletion or redaction** — the user decides how to handle them
   188→
   189→### Locale Support
   190→
   191→| Feature | Description |
   192→|---------|-------------|
   193→| Templates | English (`en`) and Simplified Chinese (`zh-cn`) |
   194→| Auto-detect | Detects language from repo content first, then system locale |
   195→| Reply language | Follows user explicit request > message language > repo locale > template locale > `en` |
   196→| Non-destructive | Never rewrites existing files just to translate them |
   197→
   198→### Environment Adaptation
   199→
   200→MemoryTree does not assume the target repo has a Python environment:
   201→
   202→```
   203→Prefer uv run python → fall back to system python → fall back to direct template scaffolding
   204→```
   205→
   206→Never auto-creates `.venv` or `pyproject.toml` inside the project.
   207→
   208→## Background Heartbeat
   209→
   210→The heartbeat automates transcript discovery, import, cleaning, commit, and push without consuming model tokens or requiring human intervention.
   211→
   212→**Execution flow**:
   213→
   214→```
   215→Acquire lock → Load config.toml → Iterate projects → Discover → Import
   216→→ Clean → Commit → (auto_push) Push → Release lock → Exit
   217→```
   218→
   219→### Install the daemon
   220→
   221→```bash
   222→memorytree-daemon install
   223→```
   224→
   225→### CLI commands
   226→
   227→| Command                     | Description |
   228→|-----------------------------|-------------|
   229→| `memorytree-daemon install`   | Register the heartbeat with the OS scheduler (cron / launchd / Task Scheduler). |
   230→| `memorytree-daemon uninstall` | Remove the scheduled heartbeat task. |
   231→| `memorytree-daemon run-once`  | Run a single heartbeat cycle immediately. |
   232→| `memorytree-daemon watch`     | Continuous loop for development and debugging only. |
   233→| `memorytree-daemon status`    | Show registration state and last execution result. |
   234→
   235→### Key settings (`~/.memorytree/config.toml`)
   236→
   237→| Setting              | Default  | Description |
   238→|----------------------|----------|-------------|
   239→| `auto_push`          | `true`   | Push to remote after each commit. Skips if no remote is configured. |
   240→| `heartbeat_interval` | `"5m"`   | Interval between heartbeat executions. |
   241→| `log_level`          | `"info"` | Log verbosity (`debug`, `info`, `warn`, `error`). |
   242→
   243→### Design constraints
   244→
   245→- Single execution, stateless, idempotent — safe to re-run at any interval
   246→- One computer = one scheduled heartbeat task
   247→- Will not silently register — always confirms before `install`
   248→
   249→For details see [`references/heartbeat-scheduling.md`](references/heartbeat-scheduling.md) and [`references/global-configuration.md`](references/global-configuration.md).
   250→
   251→## Design Principles
   252→
   253→| Principle | How |
   254→|-----------|-----|
   255→| **Zero intrusion** | Never changes the repo's branch model, CI, or review process |
   256→| **Minimal dependencies** | Scripts target Python standard library; no third-party packages required at install time |
   257→| **Deterministic** | Transcript cleaning done by code, not model tokens |
   258→| **Single source of truth** | Each concept defined in one reference file, others cross-reference |
   259→| **Contract first** | Document the spec (heartbeat, daemon) before writing code |
   260→| **User asset** | Memory belongs to the user; auto_push on by default to prevent local data loss |
   261→
   262→## Reference Docs
   263→
   264→| File | Purpose |
   265→|------|---------|
   266→| [`references/project-detection.md`](references/project-detection.md) | Detect install state |
   267→| [`references/memory-layout.md`](references/memory-layout.md) | Folder and file naming rules |
   268→| [`references/update-rules.md`](references/update-rules.md) | When to version goals, todos, and session logs |
   269→| [`references/git-policy.md`](references/git-policy.md) | Git defaults, auto-push, and policy merge |
   270→| [`references/heartbeat-scheduling.md`](references/heartbeat-scheduling.md) | Heartbeat architecture, daemon CLI, execution flow |
   271→| [`references/global-configuration.md`](references/global-configuration.md) | `~/.memorytree/` layout, `config.toml` schema, `alerts.json` format |
   272→| [`references/transcript-archive.md`](references/transcript-archive.md) | Transcript archival, cleaning, search, and upload rules |
   273→| [`references/locale-selection.md`](references/locale-selection.md) | Locale choice rules |
   274→| [`references/response-language.md`](references/response-language.md) | Reply-language precedence |
   275→| [`references/execution-environment.md`](references/execution-environment.md) | How to run scripts without a project-local Python environment |
   276→| [`references/upgrade-path.md`](references/upgrade-path.md) | Safe adoption flow for partial repos |
   277→
   278→## Update
   279→
   280→```bash
   281→cd ~/.claude/skills/memorytree-workflow && git pull
   282→```
   283→
   284→## Requirements
   285→
   286→- [Claude Code](https://claude.ai/claude-code) CLI
   287→- Python 3.11+ (for transcript and heartbeat scripts; `tomllib` is stdlib in 3.11+)
   288→- Git
   289→
   290→## License
   291→
   292→MIT
   293→
